title: Emergency Guide: Dog Choking on a Bone – What to Do Now
meta_desc: Is your dog choking on a bone? Learn the signs of a high-urgency emergency, immediate steps to take, and how to help your vet triage the situation.
content_html:
What is Airway Obstruction?

Choking occurs when a foreign object, such as a bone, becomes lodged in your dog's throat, esophagus, or trachea. This blockage can prevent your dog from breathing and may cause immediate panic. Because bones are often sharp or awkwardly shaped, they can easily get stuck and cause physical trauma to the airway.

Is it an emergency?

Yes. This is a High Urgency emergency. If your dog is gasping for air, pawing at their mouth, coughing forcefully, or if their gums are turning blue or purple, you must act immediately. Do not wait to see if the bone passes on its own, as oxygen deprivation can lead to permanent organ damage or death within minutes.

Why a photo helps triage

If you can safely see the bone without putting your fingers at risk of being bitten, a quick photo can be life-saving. Providing a photo to your veterinarian or triage assistant helps them identify the size, type, and orientation of the bone. This allows the surgical or emergency team to prepare specific instruments for extraction before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Should I try to pull the bone out with my hands?
faqs.answer: Only attempt this if the bone is clearly visible and easy to reach. Be very careful, as a choking dog may bite reflexively out of panic. If the bone feels lodged, do not force it, as you may push it deeper or cause more damage.

faqs.question: How can I perform the Heimlich maneuver on a dog?
faqs.answer: For small dogs, hold them against your chest and apply pressure to the abdomen just below the ribs. For large dogs, place your hands just below the ribcage and apply firm, quick upward thrusts while they are standing or lying on their side.

faqs.question: What if my dog stops breathing?
faqs.answer: If your dog loses consciousness and stops breathing, check the mouth for the obstruction one last time, then begin emergency rescue breaths and chest compressions (CPR) while someone else drives you to the nearest emergency veterinarian.
